item.text:
# AUTHORITATIVE SEMANTIC COMMISSION — CH-02

CH-02 owns the book’s operational boundary. Its belief move is that the reader can leave a precise behavioral category without treating sugar molecules, carbohydrates, ordinary nourishment, or clinical nutrition as the enemy. It must resolve fruit-and-carbohydrate objections, hidden-ingredient panic, household label anxiety, and attempts to exempt core sweets because they are homemade, “healthier,” comforting, celebratory, or convenient. Its authority is definitional and protective, not nutritional or diagnostic. The chapter’s freedom curve remains low and its demolition work neutral: it establishes the object of inquiry before later persuasion begins.

The target is compulsive engagement with refined or added-sugar products and the genuinely ambiguous refined-savoury edge defined below—not eating or nutrition generally. Debut the exact token `BAD SUGAR` (`M-01`). It always names the bounded category, never a person, body, child, accidental molecule, or moral condition. `GOOD SUGAR` and `BAD SUGAR` are operational method labels only; neither signifies virtue, cleanliness, worth, or the quality of a caregiver.

`GOOD SUGAR` comprises sugars and starches found in ordinary nourishment, including whole fruit, vegetables, legumes, nuts, plain dairy, oats, rice, potatoes, bread, pasta, and other ordinary meal staples. Their sugar or carbohydrate molecules do not place them inside the target.

`BAD SUGAR` has two and only two branches. The first is a product conventionally consumed as a sugar-sweetened drink, confection, dessert, sweet baked good, or added-sugar sweet snack, including products sweetened with sugar, honey, or syrup. Core examples are soda, sweetened coffee or tea, sweets, biscuits, cakes, pastries, ice cream, dessert products, and prominently sweetened cereals or bars. Hunger, care, celebration, convenience, homemade status, or a natural, organic, dark, low-sugar, or healthier label cannot exempt a core item.

The second branch is a genuinely ambiguous refined savoury snack functioning as a discretionary reward, rescue, graze, or automatic nibble rather than ordinary meal food. Behavioral-role inquiry is permitted only at this gray savoury edge. It must not become motive analysis for every food, meal scoring, or a way to exempt an unmistakable core sweet item. Small incidental ingredients do not convert an ordinary meal into `BAD SUGAR`.

The definitional decree must appear exactly:

> Throughout this book, whenever I say sugar, a sugar hit, or quitting sugar, take me to mean BAD SUGAR as defined here—not fruit, ordinary meals, or every carbohydrate.

Use `sugar hit` or `planned dose` only for intentional choices inside the line. “Dose” is rhetorical shorthand for a decision unit, never a clinical-addiction or physiological claim.

Totality means no planned `BAD SUGAR` doses inside the reader’s eventual chosen threshold. It does not mean molecular purity, dietary perfection, loss of autonomy, or a claim that every in-scope product has identical physiology. This chapter defines that destination but does not ask the reader to stage a quit or prove seriousness through immediate behavior change.

The Molecule Margin is part of the chapter’s protected boundary. A trace ingredient, uncertain sauce, accidental bite, or honest classification mistake is not a deliberate return and does not constitute failure. The reader may notice it, learn from it if useful, and continue from the choice already made. This margin covers accidents and genuine ambiguity only; it cannot be used to rename a planned exception as an accident. Define the distinction without developing the later lapse argument.

Whole fruit and ordinary meal carbohydrates remain protected. Juice, dried fruit, non-nutritive sweeteners, alcohol, caffeine, and adjacent indulgences are not required quits and do not determine success. They may become optional, separate questions later, but this chapter must not expand the current project to include them.

`SA-02` should supply the assigned image: an operational boundary works like a usable line on a map rather than a microscopic inspection of every molecule. Its argumentative job is to make precision compatible with ordinary living and to prepare the Molecule Margin. It must not introduce a permanent food-monitoring system or the later Ordinary Eating Compass.

The chapter principally serves `PER-01`, `PER-02`, and `PER-04`: the reader afraid that favorite sweetness or ordinary carbohydrates will be swept away; the restriction veteran expecting another totalizing rule system; and the household translator whose label knowledge can become hidden-sugar anxiety or moral instruction. `D-05` contributes the stance of someone who can read labels but still needs a livable boundary. That dialect is plan-authored ventriloquism, not participant testimony, and should be rendered as reader voice rather than sourced quotation.

The assigned lived evidence illustrates classification pressure only.

`S-010#E-001` comes from a peer-reviewed qualitative interview study of beverage choices among WIC-enrolled families in a southeastern US health system. After WIC nutritionist advice, one caregiver reported offering a child more water, changing the child’s drinks, and checking sugar labels. It can illustrate household translation and why usable definitions matter. It cannot establish the caregiver’s own abstinence, durability, health improvement, intervention efficacy, or a universal family prescription. If direct language is retained, the packet-cleared quotation is:

> “I started giving her water more. I started changing up her drinks. I started checking the sugars on the back.”

This is an `EXACT_QUOTE` from `S-010#C-001`, Table 3, Topic 3 “Education about Healthy Drink Choices,” Parent 03. It is exact to the published table, not the raw transcript; the article’s authors lightly edited published quotations for conjunctions and colloquialisms. Required attribution: Ali S, Gibbs SE, Wiseman K, Zoellner J, Montez K, Taxter A, Suarez M, Hindel L, Lewis KH, *Maternal and Child Health Journal* 29 (2025), 628–638, https://doi.org/10.1007/s10995-025-04075-w.

`S-011#E-001` comes from a peer-reviewed qualitative focus-group study of sweet-beverage purchasing in four low-income peri-urban communities in Accra. One shopper explained avoiding commercial sweet drinks through ingredient distrust. The fuller response reported a homemade sobolo substitution, while the study warned that traditional drinks could contain unrecognised sugar. This supports the limited point that distrust, source, label, or homemade status does not by itself settle the operational category. It does not establish abstinence, freedom, durability, or equivalence between homemade and commercial drinks. If direct language is retained, the packet-cleared quotation is:

> “I don’t really buy any (drinks). I believe they are just made of sugar and coloring…”

This is an `EXACT_QUOTE` from `S-011#C-001`, Results §3.4.2, Person 2, Teiman. The published English is a checked translation from Twi or Ga. Required attribution: Amegah C, Odei Obeng-Amoako G, Ng S, Lambon-Quayefio M, Adu-Afarwuah S, *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health* 23 (2026), 799, https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph23060799.

Neither account is outcome evidence. Do not convert household action into caregiver blame, moralize a child’s food, prescribe family behavior, or infer health effects. The observations establish that people may act on labels, advice, and product beliefs; the chapter’s classification rule comes from the canonical definition, not from these reports.

The chapter must carry a clearly bounded clinical and eating-disorder safety perimeter. Prescribed glucose, diabetes or metabolic treatment, pregnancy-related dietary advice, medical nutrition therapy, and every other qualified clinical instruction outrank the book. The book is not eating-disorder treatment; where such care is relevant, direct the reader toward qualified care rather than intensifying restriction, classification, or self-surveillance. Do not prescribe calories, carbohydrates, weight targets, fasting, meal timing, ingredient targets, or dietary purity. Promise no weight loss, metabolic repair, mental clarity, taste change, or bodily transformation.

The chapter’s instruction must appear verbatim:

> USE BAD SUGAR ONLY AS THIS BOOK DEFINES IT; NEVER APPLY THE LABEL TO WHOLE FRUIT, ORDINARY MEALS, EVERY CARBOHYDRATE, PRESCRIBED GLUCOSE, OR CLINICAL NUTRITION.

Do not turn the chapter into an argument about addiction, health danger, pleasure, reward, or efficacy, and do not initiate the behavioral crossing. It receives the book’s freedom destination and hands forward a stable, non-purity target that can be investigated without category creep. The commissioned budget is approximately 3,200 words.
